source_file: The corporate social credit system in China and its transnational impact.txt
text:
makes this inﬂuence indirect. Digital companies like communication ser-
vices or booking tools, are often border transcending. If the CSCS applies
to such digital corporations and they decide to act according to it, the cor-
poration will take the rules of the CCP with it across borders. It might no
longer be necessary to order a corporation to shut down online sessions of
participants outside of China (an action that risks a public reaction as it
was the case in the Zoom example) but corporations will know that online
events that are perceived as endangering state security will have a negative
eﬀect on the companies’ scores and they might thus choose to act pre-emp-
tively to avoid the consequences. These private actors then act according to
an emerging knowledge structure and certain conceptions of right out of
their own perceived interest.
    A certain informality can be perceived in the features of the scale
ratings as well as the features connecting diﬀerent corporate actors as
well as the individual and the corporation, as the boundaries between
law, (corporate) culture and informal agreements begin to blur. This
makes governance subtler and thus easier to transport. Informality
‘oﬀers the advantages of being more ﬂexible, speedier, less public and pol-
itically contentious, and less constraining on freedom of action’, it mini-
mises ‘the impediments to cooperation at both the domestic and
international levels’.93
    Lastly, the intrusiveness of the system makes the possibilities to obtain
legal remedies all the more important. Information on remedies or judicial
review is not comprehensive at the moment and there are no uniform stan-
dards for credit repair.94 In the ﬁeld of data protection, the lack of an inde-
pendent authority to monitor compliance to data protection regulations as
well as the lack of independent and authoritative judgements in court,
make eﬀective judicial review questionable.95 Currently, the blacklisting
system of the State Administration for Taxation has a ‘“credit repair mech-
anism” that provides for correction of or relief from the blacklist under
certain conditions’.96 With regard to the envisioned universal SCS, it
seems that a bad score and ‘shameful’ disclosed data do not remain attached
to the corporation eternally. Five years after the discredited behaviour or
after the generation of the record, the disclosed data is no longer publicly
accessible.97 Credit restoration is possible to a limited extent via
